A system is asked to explain a scientific result. One framing says its job is mainly search: assemble candidate explanations from available parts and score them. If that framing is right, progress depends on how large and navigable the combinatorial space is. But a critic shifts the pressure point. Maybe the hard part is not building candidate explanations once a problem is posed; maybe the hard part is choosing the right problem to pose in the first place. Under that objection, better assembly does not guarantee better discovery, because discovery may depend more on selecting a fruitful target than on filling in its answer.

The Einstein premise narrows this dispute. If Einstein did not require new words, then at least some major novelty can be expressed entirely with existing tokens. That weakens one common complaint against language models: that lacking new vocabulary blocks genuine originality. It does not prove the system can be original, but it removes one alleged obstacle. If novelty can arise from recombining old words, then token reuse is compatible with conceptual advance. So the critic cannot simply say, “No new tokens, therefore no real novelty.” The bottleneck must be elsewhere, if it exists.

The journal sequence clarifies where “elsewhere” might be. The movement from light-beam chasing to train simultaneity to accelerating box is not just a set of filled-in answers; it is a chain of reframings. Each stage reposes the issue under a different setup. That means there are at least two distinguishable capacities: first, elaborating within a given frame; second, inventing the next frame. The premises say it remains unclear whether the system can do the second rather than merely the first. So even if combinatorial assembly is adequate for producing explanations inside a chosen representation, the open question is whether the system can generate the representation shift itself.

The two nonsensical-looking strings matter because they are also premises and therefore part of the world. In this world, meaningful methodological claims coexist with “Cerfoil paramorphine burrah strainer miltiest” and “Cosmopolitans stickybeak counterman afibrinogenemia furrowing.” Since no interpretation is supplied, they function as uninterpreted token sequences. Their presence demonstrates that a sequence can be well-formed enough to be presented yet not obviously integrated into the surrounding argument. This reinforces the earlier point: having tokens, even unusual ones, is not the decisive issue. New or strange words alone do not produce explanatory progress; nor does the absence of new words prevent it.

So the collision of premises yields a constrained picture. Novelty may be achievable without lexical invention, making it more accessible to an LLM than some critics allow. Yet that concession does not settle the deeper challenge, because the decisive limit may lie in selecting productive problems and inventing chained reframings. The unexplained token strings underline that symbol production by itself is cheap. What remains uncertain is whether the system can move from assembling within a frame to choosing, and then transforming, the frame itself.
